title: Nonlinear Decision Rules Made Scalable by Nonparametric Liftings
abstract: Sequential decision making often requires dynamic policies, which are computationally not tractable in general. Decision rules provide approximate solutions by restricting decisions to simple functions of uncertainties. In this paper, we consider anonparametric liftingframework where the uncertainty space is lifted to higher dimensions to obtain nonlinear decision rules. Current lifting-based approaches require predetermined functions and are parametric. We propose two nonparametric liftings, which derive the nonlinear functions by leveraging the uncertainty set structure and problem coefficients. Both methods integrate the benefits from lifting and nonparametric approaches, and hence provide scalable decision rules with performance bounds. More specifically, theset-drivenlifting is constructed by finding polyhedrons within uncertainty sets, inducing piecewise-linear decision rules with performance bounds. Thedynamics-drivenlifting, on the other hand, is constructed by extracting geometric information and accounting for problem coefficients. This is achieved by using linear decision rules of the original problem, also enabling one to quantify lower bounds of objective improvements over linear decision rules. Numerical comparisons with competing methods demonstrate superior computational scalability and comparable performance in objectives. These observations are magnified in multistage problems with extended time horizons, suggesting practical applicability of the proposed nonparametric liftings in large-scale dynamic robust optimization.

article.1. Introduction: Dynamic decision making naturally emerges in a broad range of applications in operations management, economics, finance, and engineering. For example, an inventory manager needs to adjust positions across multiple products while balancing a variety of costs. The profit, however, depends on the demand, which is uncertain and often with only partially known information. In real-world settings, these adjustments are required periodically, demanding dynamic ordering decisions, in particular when demand uncertainties also vary in time. Similarly in the emerging sharing economies, such as ride sharing, determining the optimal capacity allocations across space and time is instrumental to meeting service-level goals. In real-time implementation, the capacities need to be repositioned. Because the demand can change during the process, such capacity allocations need to accommodate realized demand, hence necessitating dynamic allocation decisions. The common thread among these and similar decision-making processes is the presence of time-varying uncertainty and the need for dynamic decisions. Such problems have been studied under the framework of multistage optimization with the corresponding type of uncertainty models. When uncertainties can be described by probability distributions, stochastic optimization (SO) provides a versatile framework (Birge and Louveaux 2011,Powell 2019). When uncertainties can be described by sets, robust optimization (RO) finds a dynamic decision that protects against the worst-case realization of uncertain parameters (Ben-Tal et al. 2009,Bertsimas et al. 2011a). The broad capability of these approaches to model a wide range of dynamic decision problems is somewhat limited, because solving multistage problems to optimality has been shown to be computationally hard (Shapiro and Nemirovski 2005). For RO problems and for some classes of SO,decision rule approximationshave been studied. These methods find tractable solutions by first restricting the structure of dynamic decisions to a parametric family of functions of uncertainties and subsequently finding the corresponding optimal parameters. A popular class of such decision rules is linear decision rules (LDRs), where contingent decisions are assumed to be affine functions of the uncertain parameters (Ben-Tal et al. 2004,Kuhn et al. 2011). To improve the approximation of LDRs, several types of nonlinear decision rules have been proposed in the last decade. For example, piecewise linear, quadratic, or polynomial decision rules have been considered to allow more functional flexibility than LDRs with increased computational cost. We will provide a comprehensive review of various decision rules inSection 1.1. Despite these efforts, the intractable nature of dynamic decision making leads to significant computational costs for large problems. This renders many available nonlinear decision rule approaches challenging for implementable solutions in practice. For example, dynamic allocation of vehicles in urban transportation involves thousands of vehicles (Bertsimas et al. 2019a). In the context of inventory management, Zara introduces approximately 10,000 new designs every year (García 2014), which necessitates inventory decisions in response to high-dimensional uncertainty. Similarly, decision making in electricity market involves a large number of generators and their dynamic dispatching plans under various service requirements (Conejo et al. 2010). These examples demonstrate the desire of morescalablemethods for obtaining nonlinear decision rules. To this end, we focus on developing a scalable decision-making framework for solving multistage RO problems. Given that LDRs are widely used with its promising computational efficiency for solving real-world problems (see, e.g.,Rocha and Kuhn 2012,Lorca et al. 2016,Bandi et al. 2019), we seek to provide nonlinear decision rules whose computational costs are comparable to those of LDRs. In particular, our framework is based on the concept ofliftingthe space of uncertainties to higher dimensions, originated from the extended formulation in combinatorial optimization (Conforti et al. 2010,Fiorini et al. 2012). This approach offers an alternative path to solve RO problems; for example, ellipsoidal uncertainty sets can be approximated with polyhedrons on the lifted space (Bärmann et al. 2016). It has been adapted to develop nonlinear decision rules, where the auxiliary variables can be interpreted as nonlinear functions of the original uncertain parameters, such as their positive and negative parts (Chen et al. 2007) or piecewise-linear functions (Georghiou et al. 2015). However, as we will discuss later in detail, these approaches typically require predetermined nonlinear functions, chosen independently for problem instances. In this regard, many of existing liftings areparametric, because they rely on a priori functional structure. To complement methods for computing decision rules, we propose anonparametriclifting framework, which concurrently identifies instance-specific nonlinear functions and computes corresponding generalized decision rules. More specifically, we provide two nonparametric lifting methods, namely, theset-drivenand thedynamics-drivenliftings. By leveraging the underlying uncertainty models and constraints of optimization problems, both methods enable us to establish theoretical bounds on their performances while preserving tractability, and, hence, warrant scalable nonlinear decision rules for robust sequential decision making. Our contributions are summarized in the following: The uncertainty set-driven lifting (SL) chooses a set of points from the original uncertainty set to construct the lifted uncertainty set. For general uncertainty sets, we establish performance bounds of nonlinear decision rules that are obtained from the set-driven lifting. They also provide insights on how to choose the points to define the lifting. We discuss the detailed implementation for the case when these points constitute anL1-ball. The dynamics-driven lifting (DL) leverages both the geometry of the uncertainty set and the problem coefficients. The problem dynamics is captured by using optimal LDRs from the original uncertainty set and used to construct the corresponding lifted sets by adding linear constraints. We quantify a lower bound on the objective improvement over LDRs and provide a sufficient condition when such improvements are strict. The new concept ofnonanticipativelifting extends both of these liftings to multistage robust decision-making problems. Examples for an efficient construction of the set-driven lifting under rectangular and nonrectangular uncertainty are provided. We also present how the dynamics-driven lifting can be extended and its theoretical properties generalized to multistage settings. The performances of the two nonparametric lifting methods are numerically compared with other state-of-the-art methods. Our result from a multi-item newsvendor problem demonstrates computational scalability of the proposed lifting methods, especially for large problems where other approaches experience computational difficulty or do not improve over LDRs. The proposed methods are also applied to multiperiod inventory control, highlighting the enhanced improvements in multistage setting and their practicality for large-scale multistage problems. This paper is organized as follows. After reviewing existing decision rule results from a parametric versus nonparametric viewpoint, we set up the discussion by a preliminary introduction of a generic lifting framework for obtaining nonlinear decision rules inSection 2. The new set-driven and the dynamics-driven liftings for two-stage RO problems are then discussed inSections 3and4, respectively. We then extend both of these liftings to multistage settings inSection 5to be suited for practical settings. InSection 6, we discuss the numerical performance of our nonparametric liftings on three experiments, one for a two-stage setting (Section 6.1) and two for multistage problems (Sections 6.2and6.3). Our findings are then summarized inSection 7with further extensions. 1.1. Review of Dynamic Decision Rules We now briefly review LDRs and a set of current nonlinear decision rule approaches by dividing them into parametric and nonparametric approaches as they relate to this work. 1.1.1. Linear Decision Rules. Also known as affine policies, LDRs achieve tractability by approximating continuous dynamic decisions as affine functions of the uncertain parameters.Kuhn et al. (2011)propose a primal and dual approach for developing LDRs to provide upper and lower bounds. Their promising performance has also been evidenced with theoretical bounds, which depend mainly on the geometry of uncertainty sets. For example, LDRs are shown to be optimal for two-stage RO problems when uncertainty sets are simplices (Bertsimas and Goyal 2012). This is further generalized byBertsimas and Bidkhori (2015), establishing the performance bounds by measuring how well an uncertainty set can be approximated by a simplex. In multistage RO problems, LDRs are shown to be optimal when uncertainty sets are hypercubes (Bertsimas et al. 2010), and further generalized to lattices (Iancu et al. 2013). 1.1.2. Parametric Nonlinear Decision Rules. Lifting-based approaches typically require predetermined nonlinear functions to construct nonlinear decision rules. Therefore, they can be viewed asparametric.Chen et al. (2008)propose segregated linear decision rules (SLDRs), in which the additional variables represent positive and negative parts of uncertainties. They are further generalized byChen and Zhang (2009)as the extended affinely adjustable robust counterpart (EAARC) for uncertainty sets with linear and norm constraints.Georghiou et al. (2015)consider nonlinear decision rules by defining auxiliary variables from a variety of functions such as piecewise-linear or polynomial for polyhedral sets. For distributionally robust optimization (DRO) problems, auxiliary variables are introduced by extending the set of probability distributions into higher dimensions (Wiesemann et al. 2014,Bertsimas et al. 2019b). Parametric nonlinear decision rules without lifting include those ofSee and Sim (2010)with truncated linear functions andBertsimas et al. (2011c)with polynomial functions of a fixed degree. For quadratic decision rules (QDRs),Xu and Hanasusanto (2023)propose a copositive programming formulation that admits semidefinite approximations. 1.1.3. Nonparametric Nonlinear Decision Rules. When nonlinear functions are not given explicitly, geometric approaches have been proposed to generate partitions of uncertainty sets and assign an LDR to each piece. For a fixed number of pieces, a cutting-plane method (Bertsimas and Georghiou 2015) and a branch-and-bound algorithm (Subramanyam et al. 2020) have been proposed to find optimal partitions. Alternatively,Bertsimas and Dunning (2016)andPostek and Den Hertog (2016)provide iterative algorithms that split uncertainty sets in order to compute near-optimal decision rules. For two-stage RO problems, a class of nonparametric piecewise LDRs has also been studied by using Fourier–Motzkin elimination techniques (Zhen et al. 2018). More recently, a primal–dual lifting approach was proposed byGeorghiou et al. (2020), where a point is identified and lifted at each iteration to provide a sequence of nonparametric piecewise LDRs. 1.2. Positioning Our Approach Many existing parametric decision rule approaches achieve tractability by leveraging predetermined functional structures. Parametric lifting methods become scalable when they compute decision rules by applying LDRs to the uncertainty sets in higher dimensions. These liftings can also be extended to multistage settings (seeTable 1). However, they do not offer any theoretical guarantees on their improvements in objectives compared with LDRs. Table 1.Summary of Lifting-Based Decision Rules Table 1.Summary of Lifting-Based Decision Rules On the other hand, nonparametric methods focus primarily on identifying an effective nonlinear functional structure in order to enhance the performance of respective decision rules. Here, we propose a framework that integrates the nonparametric approach with lifting. This is achieved by first deriving the nonlinear functions by constructing lifted uncertainty sets with functions that are deduced from the uncertainty sets and problem coefficients. This enables us to establish performance guarantees on objectives, while still leveraging the benefits of parametric lifting methods, that is, their computational scalability and extendability to multistage environments. The primal–dual lifting method ofGeorghiou et al. (2020)defines the liftings in a similar nonparametric manner. Our approach, however, differs in a variety of aspects, as summarized inTable 1. The primal–dual lifting method is designed to optimally solve two-stage RO problems under polyhedral uncertainty sets over iterations. Therefore, each iteration requires complete characterization of all extreme points, and hence, to the best of our knowledge, is restricted to two-stage settings and not necessarily tractable in general. On the other hand, our proposed liftings primarily aim to compute scalable decision rules that are compatible for general conic uncertainty sets as well as extendable to multistage environments. InSection 3, we will further detail this comparison. We now formally define the concept of lifting before introducing two nonparametric liftings.

Model and Preliminaries: Lifting Fram: For modeling a dynamic decision-making process of a general character, we consider a two-stage optimization problem that is exposed to an uncertain parameterξand where the two decisionsxandyare made sequentially. In this setting, decisionxis called the first-stage (here-and-now) decision and is determined before observing the uncertainξ. Onceξrealizes, the second-stage (wait-and-see) decisionyis made. We formulate this sequential decision-making problem following the paradigm of robust optimization. That is,ξis unknown but can be assumed to reside within anuncertainty setU. Consequently, the two-stage problem can be formally written as In this problem, the first-stage decisionxis determined within a feasible convex setX⊆RNx. The second-stage decisiony∈RNycan be determined by a linear optimization problem for which the constraints are affected byxand the uncertainty realizationξ=(ξ1,…,ξN)∈RN. To emphasize this dynamic, we denote it byy(ξ), showing its dependence onξ. In such an RO problem, the goal is to findxandy(ξ)that minimize the worst-case cost whenξcan take an adversarial value within the uncertainty setU. Here, we assume thatξaffects the two-stage problem asA(ξ)=A0+∑n=1NAnξnandh(ξ)=h0+Hξ, whereh0∈RM,H∈RM×N, andAn∈RM×Nxforn=0,…,N. We also assume thatB∈RM×Ny,c∈RNx, andd∈RNyare constant. Therefore, our model admits a fixed recourse matrix, but it can still model a variety of problems in different fields such as supply chains, energy systems, and resource allocation. In order to describe the uncertain parameter in general settings, we assume that the setUis second-order conic (SOC) representable. This conic structure enables computational tractability and modeling a wide variety of uncertainties that occur in practice, such as polyhedrons, ellipsoids, and finite intersections of these. We also assume thatUis full-dimensional, nonempty, and compact. 2.1. Notation Lowercase italic is used to denote scalars, lowercase bold is used for vectors, and uppercase bold is used for matrices. Tractability via Approximations and Lifting The formulation (𝒫Adapt(U)) models a broad range of real-world problems by imposing uncertainties on the constraints. Its adaptive nature, however, makes (𝒫Adapt(U)) computationally intractable. Most prominently, LDRs have been used to overcome this challenge by imposing affine structure for the second-stage decision viay(ξ)=w+Wξ. Then finding optimal parameterswandWcan be formulated as the following single-stage RO problem: For SOC uncertainty sets, (𝒫LDR(U)) can be reformulated as a tractable second-order cone program (SOCP), which for a polyhedralU, can be reduced to a linear program (LP). Throughout this paper, we assume thatzLDR*(U)<∞, that is, there exists a feasible solution for (𝒫LDR(U)). Note that this assumption holds when (𝒫Adapt(U)) has complete recourse. However, it does not necessarily require (𝒫Adapt(U)) to have a relatively complete recourse and vice versa (Bodur and Luedtke 2022). 2.2.1. Improving Approximation with Lifting. Even though LDRs are widely used for computational tractability, their suboptimality can become significant (Bertsimas and Goyal 2012). Alternatively, a more general approach is to approximate the second-stage decision with generalized decision rules, where their formal definition is as given below. (Generalized Decision Rules). For a nonlinear functiong(ξ)with componentsgk(·):RN→R,w∈RNy,W∈RNy×N, andV∈RNy×K, ageneralized decision ruleis Consequently, anoptimal decision ruleof the form (GDR) is characterized by parameters(w*,W*,V*), which are feasible and minimize the worst-case objective for (𝒫Adapt(U)). One can observe that (GDR) reduces to LDRs whenV=0. Therefore, generalized decision rules extend LDRs by introducing nonlinear terms of uncertain parameters. The nonlinear functiong(ξ)is chosen by a decision maker and is predetermined before computing optimal parameters(w*,W*,V*). Note that our approach does not require the knowledge of the exact form ofg(ξ)a priori. Several classes of decision rules of the form (GDR) have been studied by using the notion of lifting (seeTable 1), where the uncertainty sets are enlarged into higher dimensions. In this approach, new uncertainty sets are properly constructed in higher dimensions, such that the additional variables representg(ξ). We follow this notion of lifting and define the lifted uncertainty set as follows. (Lifted Uncertainty Set). An illustrative figure ofTheorem 1withN=K= 1 is presented inFigure 1. Special cases of this proposition have been considered byGeorghiou et al. (2015)andDelage and Iancu (2015). Theorem1does not require specific structure ofg(·).That is, even for nonconvexg(·),optimal decision rules of the form(GDR)can be found by using convex uncertainty sets in the lifted space. This is becauseTheorem 1does not ensure tractability in general, because characterizing convex hull is hard. Nevertheless, it offers a geometric insight: in order to provide “good” tractable generalized decision rules, one needs to chooseg(·)that allows an efficient approximationU¯ofU¯g. This corollary demonstrates that outer the approximation ofU¯gproduces generalized decision rules that improve over LDRs. Therefore,Theorem 1andCorollary 1compose a lifting procedure for obtaining generalized decision rules: a decision maker first (i) chooses a nonlinear functiong(·), then (ii) constructs a tractable lifted setU¯containingU¯g, and finally (iii) computes the corresponding decision rule by solving (𝒫GDR(U¯)). In fact, most lifting-based nonlinear decision rules can be regarded as a synthesis of these three steps. For interested readers, Section EC.1 of the electronic companion sheds light on the parametric lifting methods listed inTable 1from this viewpoint. In the proposed set-driven and dynamics-driven liftings,g(·)is chosen from the geometry and problem data following the above three steps in order to obtain scalable decision rules. In the subsequent sections, we elaborate on how such functions are identified for generic SOC uncertainty sets and the way in which the respective efficient outer approximation ofU¯gis constructed. (Discrete Here-and-Now Decisions). Our framework can be extended to cases where (𝒫Adapt(U)) contains discrete here-and-now decisions, that is, when the setXis mixed-integer representable. In fact, the implementation of both lifting methods remains the same regardless of whether there are discrete first-stage decisions or not. The only difference is in computing decision rules via solving (𝒫LDR(U)) and (𝒫GDR(U¯)); with discrete decisions, these problems become mixed-integer linear or mixed-integer second-order conic programs, both of which can be solved by using off-the-shelf solvers. Because similar extensions typically hold for many existing decision rule approaches, we focus here on continuous here-and-now decisions in the interest of brevity.

(Comparison with Primal–Dual Lifting Method). The primal–dual lifting method ofGeorghiou et al. (2020)to derive piecewise LDRs are also based on lifting a set of points. Despite these similarities, these two methods are different in the following aspects. First, the primal–dual lifting method requiresUto be polyhedral, whereas the set-driven lifting permits SOC representableU. Second, the primal–dual lifting method requires an explicit characterization of the convex hullConv(Ξ\ΞS)at each iteration, which is NP-hard in general except in very specific cases, for example, whenExt(U)belongs to zero-one vertices (Angulo et al. 2015,Georghiou et al. 2020). Therefore, the primal–dual lifting method does not warrant computational tractability for obtaining piecewise-linear decision rules. On the other hand, the set-driven lifting relies on finding an inner polyhedron with a few extreme points, and, hence, generalized decision rules can be efficiently computed. Last, the primal–dual lifting method aims to optimally solve two-stage RO problems only. Special Case withL1-Balls We now consider the special case when the set-driven lifting can be constructed withL1-balls. To this end, we obtain the class ofL1-linear decision rules(L1-LDRs), where a cross-polytope is used for the set-driven lifting. LetPL1(P,q)={Pξ+q∈RN:‖ξ‖1≤1}be an affine transformation of the standardL1-ball for a nonsingular matrixP∈RN×Nandq∈RN. WhenPL1(P,q)⊆Uand with the dilation factorλ, the corresponding set-driven lifted setU¯SL(PL1(P,q))is constructed fromDefinition 4as FromCorollary 2andExample 1, one can show that the above set is associated with a nonlinear functiongL1(ξ)≔[P−1(ξ−q)]−. Therefore, solving a problem𝒫GDR(U¯SL(PL1(P,q)))yields anL1-LDR of the form For this piecewise LDR, note that even though its number of pieces is exponential (at most2N, because of component-wise negative parts), it allows closed-form expressions. Moreover, these decision rules are obtained by solving a static RO problem whose computational tractability is comparable to the problem (𝒫LDR(U)). This highlights thatL1-LDRs achieve simplicity as well as tractability, both of which are desirable characteristics in many decision-making settings. Because the set (2) is a special case of the set-driven lifting,L1-LDRs also have the same a priori performance bounds as inTheorem 2. This implies that it is necessary to find a cross-polytope, inscribed inU, that minimizes the dilation factor in order to achieve the best performance bounds. Because identifying such a desired polyhedron is known to be challenging, we provide a method to efficiently obtainL1-balls in many practical uncertainty sets, as illustrated in electronic companion, Section EC.2, with concrete examples. In particular, we show that for ellipsoid uncertainty sets, the dilation factor ofL1-balls leads to the same bounds that can be (also geometrically) derived fromJohn’s (2014)ellipsoid, whereas simplices do not (see Example EC.3 in the electronic companion). 3.3.1. Comparison with EAARC. The closed-form expression (3) ofL1-LDRs implies that whenP=κI(κ>0without loss of generality) andq=0, it reduces to affine functions ofξand[ξ]−. Because the positive part can be expressed as[ξ]+=ξ−[ξ]−, it implies that EAARC inTable 1, both using[ξ]+and[ξ]−, can be interpreted asL1-LDRs withP=κIandq=0. Although other algebraic lifting approaches can also provide policies of similar structure, they require more restrictive assumptions on their uncertainty models (seeTable 1). Because our goal is to study more general settings, here we compare the performance ofL1-LDRs with EAARC only, which is developed with more general uncertainty sets of the form In the next proposition, we present a sufficient condition for the equivalence betweenL1-LDRs (withP=κIandq=0) and EAARC. For this, letU¯EA,SL≔U¯SL(PL1(κI,0))be the set-driven lifted uncertainty set ofUEAfromPL1(κI,0). If p= 1, thenzEA*=zGDR*(U¯EA,SL).Otherwise,zEA*≤zGDR*(U¯EA,SL). Proposition 2shows that EAARC performs equally to theL1-LDRs whenUEAis described with theL1-norm, and it improves for any other norms. Note that this result holds only when the set-driven lifting is constructed from cross-polytopes of the formPL1(κI,0). Because theL1-LDRs can be implemented with any cross-polytopes, it is capable to outperform EAARC when such balls are constructed in order to minimize the dilation factor.Figure 4provides the corresponding geometric insight. In the example of a tilted ellipsoidal uncertainty set, if a standardL1-ball is used for the set-driven lifting, its dilation factor increases withR. However, if theL1-ball is chosen by the axis of the ellipsoid, then its dilation factor is constant with2. Therefore, asRincreases, EAARC becomes more conservative thanL1-LDRs. In general, finding a cross-polytope that minimizes the dilation factor may be intractable. In the electronic companion, Section EC.2, we discuss efficient approximations of such polytopes can be obtained. 3.3.2. Summary. So far, we have discussed the set-driven lifting method. Inspired from SimpLifting for polyhedrons, we define a tractable lifting approach for SOC uncertainty sets by using an inner polyhedron. As a special case, the set-driven lifting withL1-balls is discussed along with its practical implementation as well as its connection to a class of nonlinear decision rules in the literature. When decision makers have insufficient information to select the points, this set-driven lifting may not perform well. This also can occur when the corresponding lifted sets are constructed with large dilation factors. For these settings, we next develop an alternative lifting technique that exploits both the geometry and structure of the problem.

Dynamics-Driven Lifting: The set-driven lifting in the previous section constructs lifted uncertainty sets by using the geometry ofU, and the performance bounds fromTheorem 2suggest to choose an inner polyhedron that minimizes the dilation factor. Therefore, the set-driven lifting is independent of the coefficients in constraints and in the objective function of the two-stage problem (𝒫Adapt(U)). This independence renders the set-driven lifting less effective, as demonstrated next. Consider a two-stage RO problem zEx2*=2N,andzLDR,Ex2*=2Nwith the unique optimal LDR given byyLDR,Ex2*(ξ)=e. This observation shows that LDRs perform poorly for this example, where its suboptimality increases with the rateO(N). To improve this, consider using the set-driven lifting via theL1-ball. Indeed, such a cross-polytope can be found that recovers the optimal objective value, as we formally state in the following observation. The set-driven lifting with the standard L1-ball provides an optimal decision rule. Although the identifiedL1-ball yields an optimal decision rule, the symmetry ofUEx2might suggest that we could expect similar results when using other cross-polytopes with the same dilation factor. However, in the next observation, we claim that this conjecture is not true. There exists an L1-ball with the same dilation factor as the standard L1-ball, such that the corresponding set-driven lifting cannot improve over LDRs. Observation 5implies that using the geometry of uncertainty sets only is not sufficient to improve over LDRs. To this end, we next develop an alternative lifting method that leverages both the set geometry and the problem structure. The proofs of these observations are relegated to the electronic companion, Section EC.5. 4.1. Extracting Information from Constraints To construct the dynamics-driven lifting, we use an optimal LDR to extract geometric information from constraints of the two-stage problem. Let(x*,w*,W*)be an optimal LDR obtained by solving the problem (𝒫LDR(U)). Then, under this solution, each robust constraint in (𝒫LDR(U)) is satisfied with a linear inequality inξ∈U. The dynamics-driven lifting endeavors to leverage these functions{fm+bm⊤ξ:m∈[M]}that are induced from the optimal LDR. In particular, it is defined by first characterizing the nonlinear functionsg(·)from{fm+bm⊤ξ:m∈[M]}and constructing its associated lifted uncertainty setU¯DL(g)⊇U¯gin a tractable fashion. A set ofbasis vectors{v(n)∈RNy|n∈N}defines the dynamics-driven lifting. Although these vectors can be chosen arbitrarily for now, we will later provide a guideline on how to choose them. This requirement seems analogous to other parametric approaches for developing piecewise-linear decision rules, for example, when using segmentation of uncertainty space (Georghiou et al. 2015) or employing copositive programming (Xu and Hanasusanto 2023). Comparison with Existing Methods As opposed to many of existing nonlinear decision rules, whose improvements over LDRs are established only byTheorem 1and thus may not be strict, the proposed dynamics-driven lifting provides a sufficient condition for strict improvement. We consider this difference to primarily come from the fact that dynamics-driven lifting leverages information extracted from the optimal LDR, which is not incorporated in other lifting-based methods. Other tractable non-lifting-based methods, such as LP and semidefinite program (SDP) approximations for RO problems with sums of piecewise-linear convex functions (Ardestani-Jaafari and Delage 2016) or linearized robust counterparts (LRCs;Ardestani-Jaafari and Delage 2021) for two-stage problems, do not have such theoretical guarantees on strict improvement in general. In fact,Ardestani-Jaafari and Delage (2021)provide some specific instances where their method strictly improves over LDRs. Our result, however, can be applied to any two-stage RO problems of the form (𝒫Adapt(U)). There exists also a class of nonlinear decision rules that are developed by capturing geometric information from the optimal LDRs via partitioning the uncertainty space (Bertsimas and Dunning 2016,Postek and Den Hertog 2016). In these approaches,pointsin the uncertainty set are chosen from the second-stage constraints in order to generate partitions. These points are used to provide anecessarycondition for improvement. In contrast, the dynamics-driven lifting leverageshyperplanesfrom the optimal LDRs, which enable us to establish asufficientcondition for improvement. 4.5. Summary The dynamics-driven lifting finds a set of nonlinear functions to construct lifted uncertainty sets by leveraging both the uncertainty set and constraints from optimal LDRs. This method maintains tractability by designing such sets with upper bound constraints, that allow us to establish a sufficient condition for strict improvement over LDRs.
article.5. Extensions to Multistage Problems: Two-stage optimization problems describe sequential decision-making processes by assuming that all uncertain parameters are realized at once. However, in many real-world applications such as resource allocation and inventory management, uncertainties realize at different timest∈[T], and along with these realizations, contingent decisions are made sequentially. These generalized decision-making problems can be formulated as a(T+1)-stage RO problem of the form In this formulation, for eacht,ξt∈RNtis an uncertain parameter that is realized at staget+1, andyt∈R+Nyis a decision made after observingξtat staget+ 1. Decisionyt(ξ[t])isnonanticipative, that is, it depends only on the previously realized uncertaintiesξ[t]=(ξ1,…,ξt). For notational convenience, we assume that the dimension ofytis fixed asNyand the number of constraints at each stage is equal toM. That meansAt(ξ[t])∈RM×Nx,Bt,τ∈RM×Ny, andht(ξ[t])∈RMwithout loss of generality. The uncertain parameterξ[T]∈RN1+⋯+NTrealizes within a multistage uncertainty setU[T]. article.6. Numerical Experiments: To obtain additional insights on the proposed nonparametric liftings, we conduct computational experiments. We first compare our two lifting methods with other state-of-the-art techniques on multi-item newsvendor problems. This experiment provides additional insights on how the structure of uncertainty sets can affect the performance of our lifting techniques and other available approaches. We then apply our findings to multi-item, multiperiod inventory control problems to demonstrate the performance of our liftings for multistage problems. To demonstrate their performance and computational scalability independent on data subtleties, we use realistic-sized synthetic data for both problems. We then apply our methods to an instance considered byBertsimas and Georghiou (2015), which allows us to evaluate performance of our lifting methods for a class of multistage problems. All optimization problems are implemented in Julia 1.0 (Bezanson et al. 2017) using the JuMP interface (Dunning et al. 2017). The computation is conducted on an Intel Core 4.00 GHz quad-core PC with 16 GB of random access memory. All LPs, SOCPs, and MIPs (e.g., for computing bound parameters of the dynamics-driven lifting) are solved with Gurobi 9.0, and SDPs (for implementation of some of other methods) are solved with MOSEK 9.3. Run times of all methods capture all necessary steps for obtaining final solutions, including formulation of associated optimization problems and solving offline subproblems (e.g., computing LDRs and bound parameters in dynamics-driven lifting). 6.1. Multi-Item Newsvendor Problem In this problem, a decision maker needs to replenish inventories in order to satisfy customers’ demand of multiple itemsi∈[I]. The demandξiis uncertain and realizes after the ordersiis placed at a purchasing cost ofciper unit. Once the demand is fulfilled, the decision maker faces a holding costhiper unit of the leftover stock or a shortage costpiper unit of any unsatisfied demand. The decision maker needs to determine an optimal order quantitys=[si]i∈[I]that minimizes the worst-case cost. This implies that our proposed liftings are preferable for solving large RO problems when time limits are imposed. (Further Improvements). Both set-driven and dynamics-driven liftings obtain nonlinear decision rules via computing linear decision rules from lifted uncertainty sets. Summary In summary, the proposed set-driven and dynamics-driven liftings are demonstrated to be computationally more scalable than existing methods, in both two-stage and multistage settings. Our result on multi-item newsvendor problem shows that the set-driven lifting can be preferred over the dynamics-driven lifting for uncertainty sets where the inscribing polyhedrons can be chosen with small dilation factors. On the other hand, the dynamics-driven lifting performs more consistently for various uncertainty sets. For multistage problems, both liftings significantly improve objective values over LDRs with a mild increase of computation times compared with existing methods. Based on these results, we conclude that the proposed nonparametric liftings can provide scalable nonlinear decision rules for large-scale multistage RO problems.
article.7. Conclusion: In this work, we propose a nonparametric lifting framework for developing scalable nonlinear decision rules for multistage decision-making problems under uncertainty. This framework integrates the benefit of lifting the uncertainty into higher dimensions with the advantages of nonparametric approaches that extract information from uncertainty sets and problem instances. Therefore, our framework provides problem-specific nonlinear decision rules that capture the dynamics of the underlying problem directly and are fortified by theoretical performance guarantees and extendability to multistage settings. The framework consists of two types of lifting methods. The set-driven lifting is constructed by leveraging the extreme points of uncertainty sets. For this, we lift the inscribing polyhedrons and dilate them in order to obtain computationally tractable decision rules along with performance bounds. This informs decision makers on how to choose the polyhedrons. The dynamics-driven lifting is constructed by extracting geometric information from the optimal linear decision rule of the original problem. We propose a tractable procedure to lift with linear constraints and provide a sufficient condition in which the dynamics-driven lifting strictly improves objectives over LDRs. We also show that both liftings are naturally suited for multistage settings. To demonstrate the performance, we conduct three sets of numerical experiments. For multi-item newsvendor problems, the proposed nonparametric lifting framework demonstrates computational scalability, when compared with other state-of-the-art methods. For multiperiod, multi-item inventory control problems, these observations are magnified. In the last experiment, we implement our approach on the instance of a multistage problem used byBertsimas and Georghiou (2015). It reveals significant improvements in relative optimality gaps. These experiments suggest practical applicability of our nonparametric liftings for large-scale multistage robust optimization problems. We anticipate that the proposed liftings to be extended in multiple directions. For example, one can consider an algorithmic variant of both nonparametric liftings where they are updated over iterations in order to obtain better nonlinear decision rules. Alternatively, given that inner polyhedrons (for the set-driven lifting) and basis vectors (for the dynamics-driven lifting) can be viewed as hyperparameters of the proposed framework, studying an optimization framework for choosing these parameters can yield further insights. Last, combining our approach with various lifting techniques from the extended formulation literature has the potential of a new direction for developing nonparametric nonlinear decision rules.
